data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.a: All values are per serving — check the number of servings per container.
Antioxidant, protects cell membranes. Sources: nuts, seeds, vegetable oils. Deficiency: rare, causes hemolytic anemia.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.b: Based on 2,000 calorie diet. 5% DV or less = low, 20% DV or more = high.
Blood clotting (synthesizes clotting factors II, VII, IX, X). Sources: leafy greens, liver. Deficiency: bleeding disorders. Note: interacts with warfarin.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.c: Calcium absorption, bone health, immune modulation. Sources: sunlight, fortified milk, fatty fish. Deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults).
Collagen synthesis, wound healing, immune function, iron absorption. Deficiency: scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing).
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.d: DNA synthesis, RBC formation. Critical in pregnancy — deficiency causes neural tube defects.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.f: Fiber, vitamin D, calcium, iron, potassium.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.l: Listed by weight in descending order — first ingredient is present in the greatest amount.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.n: Nerve function, RBC maturation. Deficiency: pernicious/megaloblastic anemia, neuropathy.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.s: Saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, added sugars.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.t: Total energy per serving. General guide: 40 calories = low, 100 = moderate, 400+ = high.
data.pre_nursing_nutrition_foundations.v: Vision (especially night vision), immune function, skin integrity. Sources: liver, carrots, sweet potatoes. Deficiency: night blindness, xerophthalmia.

pages.preNursing.theBrainstemControlsAut: The brainstem controls autonomic cardiovascular and respiratory responses. The medulla detects changes in blood pH and CO₂ levels, triggering compensatory breathing adjustments.
pages.preNursing.theKidneysRegulateFluid: The kidneys regulate fluid balance, electrolytes, and acid-base status by adjusting reabsorption and secretion in the nephrons. Renal compensation takes 24-48 hours to take full effect.

preNursing.mod.researchStatisticsDesc: Evidence-based practice, study design, statistical literacy

preNursing.pageSubtitle: Build the essential knowledge you need before nursing school. Interactive visual lessons designed to make foundational concepts stick.
prenursing.cell-bio-active-transport: Requires ATP energy. Moves AGAINST concentration gradient. Example: Na+/K+ pump (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in).
prenursing.cell-bio-desc: Explore the building blocks of the human body through interactive diagrams and concept checks.
prenursing.cell-bio-human-cell-content: Every cell contains specialized structures called organelles that work together to maintain life. Understanding cell structure is the foundation for understanding how diseases affect the body at the cellular level.
prenursing.cell-bio-human-cell-subtitle: Identify key organelles and their functions
prenursing.cell-bio-passive-transport: No energy needed. Moves DOWN concentration gradient. Examples: diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion.
prenursing.cell-bio-transport-principle: Movement across cell membranes follows concentration gradients unless energy is invested to move against them.
prenursing.cell-bio-transport-subtitle: How substances move in and out of cells
prenursing.cellStructureDesc: Click each point to reveal the organelle name. Can you identify all 12?
prenursing.patho-desc: Learn to think like a clinician: trace disease mechanisms, recognize compensation, and differentiate early from late signs.
prenursing.patho-disease-content: Every disease is a story of homeostasis being disrupted. The body compensates to maintain function, but eventually those mechanisms fail. Understanding this progression is the key to clinical reasoning.
prenursing.patho-exam-trap: Exam questions often test whether you understand early (compensated) vs late (decompensated) signs. Example: early shock shows tachycardia (compensation) → late shock shows hypotension (decompensation).
prenursing.pharm-agonist: Binds to receptor and ACTIVATES it. Mimics the natural ligand. Example: Morphine (opioid agonist)
prenursing.pharm-antagonist: Binds to receptor and BLOCKS it. Prevents the natural ligand from activating. Example: Naloxone (opioid antagonist)
prenursing.pharm-desc: Understand how drugs interact with the body at the receptor level: the foundation for medication safety.
prenursing.pharm-receptor-clinical: Knowing whether a drug is an agonist or antagonist helps predict both therapeutic effects and side effects.
prenursing.pharm-receptor-content: Most drugs work by binding to receptors on cells. The drug-receptor interaction determines whether the drug activates or blocks a cellular response.
prenursing.pharmacokineticsSubtitle: What the body does to the drug
prenursing.phys-desc: Understand how the body maintains balance through feedback loops, fluid management, and acid-base regulation.
prenursing.phys-ecf: Outside cells. Includes intravascular (plasma) and interstitial (between cells). Contains Na+, Cl⁻, HCO3⁻.
prenursing.phys-feedback-concept: Blood glucose regulation is the classic example. Eating raises glucose → pancreas releases insulin → glucose enters cells → levels drop → insulin secretion decreases.
prenursing.phys-feedback-content: Most physiological regulation uses negative feedback. The body detects a change, activates a response, and reverses the change to restore balance.
prenursing.phys-fluid-memory: 60-40-20 rule: 60% body weight is water, 40% is ICF, 20% is ECF.
prenursing.phys-icf: Inside cells. Contains K+, Mg2+, PO4³⁻. The largest fluid compartment.
prenursing.term-desc: Decode clinical language by mastering the building blocks: prefixes, suffixes, and root words.
prenursing.term-how-content: Most medical terms are combinations of prefixes (word parts added to the beginning), root words (the core identifying the body part), and suffixes (word endings indicating a condition or procedure).
components.interactiveLearning.arrangeTh: Arrange the steps in the correct physiological order:
components.interactiveLearning.notQuiteR: Not quite right. Review the order and try again.
components.interactiveLearning.selectAll: Select all findings that are ABNORMAL
